options: Freeze early layers, fine-tune later layers with low learning rate to prevent catastrophic forgetting
Remove pre-trained weights and train from scratch
Train all layers with high learning rate
Use only ImageNet predictions without further training
explain: Transfer learning on small datasets (1,000 images) works best by freezing early layers that capture general features (edges, textures) and fine-tuning later layers with a low learning rate. Training all layers from scratch (option B) on 1,000 images leads to severe overfitting.

q: An ML engineer deploys models to both SageMaker endpoints and mobile devices (using SageMaker Edge). Model versions must be kept synchronized. How can this be managed?
options: Manual file transfer
Register models in SageMaker Model Registry; use version tags to sync both endpoints and Edge devices
Deploy separately to each platform
No synchronization needed
explain: SageMaker Model Registry stores a single model version deployable to both cloud endpoints and edge devices (via SageMaker Neo compilation), ensuring synchronized versions. Manual file transfer (option A) creates version drift and governance gaps.

q: A recommendation model is deployed. After 3 months, A/B test results show: new model CTR=3.5%, old model CTR=3.2%. Is the new model statistically significantly better?
options: No, old model is better
Yes, 0.3% difference is significant
Need to check statistical significance with confidence intervals and sample size; 0.3% difference may be noise
Statistics don't matter for recommendations
explain: A 0.3% CTR difference requires statistical hypothesis testing (chi-squared or z-test for proportions) with adequate sample size to determine significance. With insufficient traffic, 0.3% may be noise; with large traffic (millions), it can be statistically significant.

q: An organization collects real-time streaming sensor data from manufacturing equipment. Data arrives with occasional gaps (equipment downtime) and missing values. How should this be handled in the data lake?
options: Store missing values as null without handling
Reject all records with missing values
Impute missing values using forward-fill (carry forward last value) or interpolation; mark downtime periods explicitly
Use only complete records
explain: Forward-fill (LOCF) or linear interpolation preserves temporal continuity in sensor data during equipment downtime, while explicit downtime indicator features prevent the model from treating missing data as normal operating conditions.

q: An ML team uses AWS Glue Catalog to store metadata for training data. Different teams add new datasets daily. How should data governance be enforced?
options: Manual governance by one person
Governance is not needed for Glue
Define naming conventions, tagging policies, and access controls in Glue Catalog; use Lake Formation for fine-grained permissions
No governance; anyone can add anything
explain: AWS Lake Formation provides fine-grained, centralized governance for Glue Catalog data — column-level security, data filters, and tag-based access control enforce policies across all teams. Glue Catalog alone has no governance enforcement mechanism (option B).

q: An ML engineer notices that a model's performance on the training set is 90% but on validation set drops to 75%. This gap indicates a specific issue. What is the most likely cause?
options: Overfitting; model learned training data specifics rather than generalizable patterns
Underfitting due to model simplicity
The loss function is incorrect
The model is too large
explain: A 15% training-validation gap (90% train vs. 75% validation) is the classic overfitting signature — the model learned training data specifics instead of generalizable patterns. Underfitting (option B) would show both training and validation accuracy being low.

q: An ML engineer trains a language model using distributed data parallelism across 8 GPU instances. After synchronizing gradients, one instance is significantly slower. How should this be addressed?
options: Ignore the slower instance
Identify and fix straggler (network issue, resource contention); replace instance if hardware-faulty
Use only 7 instances
Stop training
explain: In synchronous distributed training, all workers must complete a step before gradient synchronization, making the slowest worker (straggler) the bottleneck. Identifying the cause (network issue, hardware fault, data loading imbalance) and fixing it prevents GPU idle time.

q: An ML engineer deploys a SageMaker endpoint but notices that for some requests, the response time is 10ms while for others it's 5 seconds. This inconsistency is problematic for real-time applications. What could cause this?
options: Normal variance in network latency
Endpoint is hitting resource limits and queuing requests; some hit cold starts; need to increase capacity or auto-scaling
The model is non-deterministic
This is expected behavior
explain: High latency variance on SageMaker endpoints typically indicates resource saturation or auto-scaling cold starts; requests hitting warmed instances get <10ms while requests triggering new instance launches experience multi-second delays. Increasing minimum instance count resolves this.

q: An ML-based system processes patient medical records for diagnosis support. A clinician notices the model occasionally makes counterintuitive recommendations. The team should ensure explainability. What should be logged?
options: No additional logging needed
Only predictions and outcomes
Model architecture only
Input features, key factors influencing prediction, confidence scores, and reasoning for clinical validation
explain: Clinical explainability requires logging the features that drove each prediction, their contribution weights, confidence scores, and model version — enabling clinicians to validate counterintuitive recommendations and regulators to audit the decision trail.

q: An organization has raw customer feedback text in multiple languages (English, Spanish, French). The text will train a multilingual sentiment classifier. What preprocessing step is critical?
options: Remove all non-English text
Combine all languages without marking
Use language-agnostic preprocessing only
Detect language for each text; apply language-specific tokenization and normalization; consider language as a feature
explain: Language-specific tokenization is critical — Spanish and French have different morphology, stop words, and tokenization rules. Language as a feature enables the classifier to learn language-specific sentiment patterns. Removing non-English text (option A) discards valuable training signal.

q: An ML engineer trains a neural network model using SGD with momentum=0.9. Training shows good convergence initially but oscillates near the minimum. How can this be improved?
options: Remove momentum
Use larger learning rate
Switch to Adam optimizer which adapts learning rate per parameter, potentially reducing oscillations
Increase momentum to 0.95
explain: Adam optimizer combines momentum and RMSprop, adapting learning rates per parameter based on gradient history, making it more robust to oscillations near minima than fixed-momentum SGD. Removing momentum (option A) would make optimization less stable, not more.

q: An ML engineer exports a trained model to ONNX format for deployment on edge devices. The original model was trained in PyTorch with GPU operations (CUDA). What validation is needed?
options: No validation; ONNX export is transparent
Skip ONNX and use PyTorch directly on devices
Test ONNX model output matches PyTorch on CPU; validate edge device inference accuracy and latency
Validation is not necessary
explain: ONNX export can introduce numerical differences through operator approximations or unsupported operations; validating output agreement between PyTorch (reference) and ONNX (deployed) on a test set ensures the export is numerically faithful before edge deployment.

q: An ML model deployed 2 years ago has never been retrained. The business reports that model performance has slowly degraded. What should the team do?
options: Deploy a completely different model
Immediately retrain without understanding why
Continue using the old model
Implement data/concept drift monitoring; retrain if drift detected; establish retraining schedule
explain: Two years without retraining is high risk for concept/data drift; implementing SageMaker Model Monitor establishes baseline and detects drift, informing targeted retraining. Immediate retraining without diagnosis (option B) may not address the actual degradation cause.

q: An ML engineer applies Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to reduce 1,000 features to 100 principal components. This significantly reduces training time. Is there a risk?
options: PCA always improves model accuracy
PCA components are harder to interpret; original feature importance is lost; ensure variance explained is sufficient (>95%)
No risk; more features are always removed
Dimensionality reduction has no tradeoffs
explain: PCA transforms features into orthogonal components that lose their original meaning, creating interpretability challenges. Ensuring >95% variance explained minimizes information loss in the transformation while achieving computational efficiency.

q: An ML engineer trains a classification model and observes that the learning curve shows training and validation accuracy are both low even with more data. What does this indicate?
options: The model has high bias (underfitting); model is too simple for the task
The model is overfitting
The data is perfect
More data alone will solve it
explain: When both training and validation accuracy are low (and don't improve with more data), the model lacks capacity to fit even the training distribution — classic high bias/underfitting. Increasing model complexity (deeper architecture) addresses this; adding more data alone does not.

q: An ML team deploys models using SageMaker Pipelines with automated retraining triggered monthly. A retraining run fails. What should happen?
options: Disable retraining
Configure pipeline to alert on failure; manually investigate and fix before retraining continues
Continue using last successful model without alerting
Deploy the broken model anyway
explain: SageMaker Pipelines should be configured with CloudWatch alarms and SNS notifications for execution failures, enabling the team to investigate root causes (data quality issues, training bugs, infrastructure failures) before rerunning. Deploying a broken model (option D) is never appropriate.

q: An organization's training data has 5 million records. An ML engineer needs to create a data sample for rapid model prototyping. Should they use random sampling or stratified sampling?
options: Use stratified sampling if target variable is imbalanced; stratified ensures minority class remains represented in sample
Sampling is unnecessary
Random sampling is always fine
Use only majority class for speed
explain: Stratified sampling ensures the prototype dataset maintains the same class distribution as the full dataset, critical for imbalanced targets where random sampling may exclude rare events entirely. Random sampling (option C) can produce unrepresentative samples for prototype testing.

q: An ML model for customer churn prediction is in production. The business asks: 'How confident are we in the model's churn predictions?' How should this be quantified?
options: Tell them accuracy percentage
Tell them the model is trained
Track calibration: compare predicted probability vs actual churn rate; well-calibrated models have reliable confidence scores
Confidence cannot be measured
explain: Model calibration measures whether predicted probabilities match empirical outcome rates — a well-calibrated model predicting 80% churn should have 80% of those customers actually churn. Reliability diagrams or Brier scores quantify calibration quality for stakeholder confidence.

q: An ML engineer processes images for training but discovers that some images are corrupted (truncated PNG files). How should these be handled?
options: Detect and remove corrupted images before training to prevent errors and ensure data quality
Store corrupted images separately
Convert all to a standard format that accepts corruption
Include them and let the model handle errors
explain: Corrupted image files (truncated PNGs) cause training failures or produce garbage gradients when decoded; detecting and removing them before training ensures a clean, reproducible training run. Training frameworks may silently skip or crash on corrupt files.

q: An ML engineer trains a seq2seq model for machine translation. The model shows good BLEU score on in-domain test data but poor performance on out-of-domain text. What technique can help?
options: This is expected and acceptable
Increase model size arbitrarily
Only deploy on in-domain data
Use domain adaptation: train on mix of in-domain and out-of-domain data; or fine-tune on target domain
explain: Domain adaptation trains on a mix of in-domain and out-of-domain data, teaching the model to generalize across both distributions. Alternatively, fine-tuning on target-domain data adapts the model to the new distribution without sacrificing in-domain performance.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model for real-time fraud detection. Latency requirement is <100ms. The model runs inference in 50ms on ml.p3 GPU instances but costs $3/hour. CPU instances run inference in 150ms for $0.10/hour. What should be used?
options: Latency doesn't matter
Use GPU instances despite cost
Use neither
Use CPU if business can tolerate 150ms; otherwise optimize model (quantization, distillation) to meet latency on cheaper instance
explain: If latency SLA permits 150ms, CPU instances are 30x cheaper and sufficient; if not, model optimization (quantization to INT8, layer pruning, knowledge distillation) can reduce GPU-only models to CPU-viable latency without sacrificing all accuracy.

q: A production ML system tracks 50 metrics in CloudWatch. The team is overwhelmed with alerts. How should alerting be prioritized?
options: Alert on every metric
Define critical metrics (accuracy, latency, error rate) with meaningful thresholds; alert only on actionable anomalies
Disable all alerts to reduce noise
Random alerting
explain: Critical metrics (prediction accuracy, P99 latency, error rate) with meaningful thresholds generate actionable alerts; alerting on all 50 metrics creates noise that drowns out real issues. CloudWatch alarm fatigue reduction improves on-call effectiveness.

q: An ML engineer trains a recommendation model on user-item interactions. New users with no interaction history appear in production. How should this cold-start problem be addressed?
options: Exclude new users entirely
Use content-based features, popularity-based recommendations, or hybrid approaches for cold-start
The model cannot handle new users
Train a separate model for new users
explain: Cold-start mitigation uses content-based filtering (item features) or popularity-based recommendations until sufficient interaction history exists for collaborative filtering. Excluding new users (option A) loses acquisition value.

q: An ML engineer compares two models: Model A uses all 100 features, Model B uses 10 hand-selected features. Model A achieves 85% accuracy, Model B achieves 83%. Which is preferable for production?
options: Both are equally good
Accuracy is the only metric that matters
Always use Model A (higher accuracy)
Model B may be preferable: lower feature complexity, faster inference, easier to explain; 2% accuracy loss may be acceptable
explain: Model B with 10 features is simpler, faster to serve (fewer feature computations), easier to explain for regulatory compliance, and less likely to overfit. The 2% accuracy tradeoff may be entirely acceptable for real-time production systems.

q: An ML team uses GitOps to manage SageMaker Pipeline definitions stored in Git. A developer merges a pipeline change that breaks the pipeline. How should this be prevented?
options: No validation; deploy directly from Git
Implement CI/CD pipeline to validate and test definitions before merging; use pull request reviews
No version control for pipelines
Manual deployment only
explain: CI/CD validation runs pipeline definition linting, unit tests on step logic, and end-to-end pipeline tests on staging data before merging. Pull request reviews catch logical errors; automated checks catch syntax and schema validation failures.

q: A machine learning model's predictions are used for high-stakes decisions (loan approval). The team must explain individual decisions to customers and regulators. What is the best approach?
options: Provide only final prediction
Use SageMaker Clarify to generate SHAP explanations showing feature importance for each prediction
Model decisions are unexplainable
Provide raw model weights
explain: SageMaker Clarify generates SHAP values for each prediction showing individual feature contributions, creating the audit-ready, per-decision explanations required by lending regulations. Raw model weights (option D) are not decision-level explanations.

q: An ML engineer needs to extract numerical features from unstructured text (e.g., product reviews) for a regression model. What approach should be used?
options: Extract features: text length, sentiment scores, keyword presence, TF-IDF vectors, or embeddings from pre-trained models
Text cannot be used for regression
Use raw text directly in numeric model
Ignore text and use only structured features
explain: Text features for regression include TF-IDF vectors (word frequency statistics), sentiment scores, text length, keyword presence, and pre-trained embeddings from models like BERT — all producing numerical representations compatible with standard ML algorithms.

q: An ML engineer applies log transformation to a right-skewed feature (e.g., income distribution). This makes the feature approximately normal. Why is this useful?
options: Log transformation is always necessary
Many algorithms (linear regression, logistic regression) perform better with normalized distributions; log transformation normalizes skewed data
Normal distribution has no benefits
Normalization has no effect on models
explain: Log transformation normalizes right-skewed distributions (like income) toward Gaussian, satisfying normality assumptions of linear models and improving coefficient estimates. Tree-based models are scale-invariant and do not benefit from log transformation.

q: An ML engineer trains a model with batch normalization. During training, batch norm uses moving averages of mean/variance. During inference, batch norm uses population statistics. Why this difference?
options: Population statistics are used during training
Batch norm has no effect on inference
Training uses batch statistics (varying per batch); inference uses accumulated population statistics for deterministic predictions
No difference; same statistics are used
explain: Batch normalization maintains running estimates of mean/variance during training (computed per batch); at inference, it uses population-wide statistics accumulated during training to ensure deterministic, stable predictions regardless of batch size.

q: An ML engineer needs to deploy a 5GB model to SageMaker endpoints. The endpoint should start within 30 seconds of receiving the first request. Is this feasible?
options: Endpoints always meet latency SLA
No; loading 5GB takes >30s; use provisioned endpoints with pre-loaded models or reduce model size via quantization/distillation
Load time is irrelevant
Yes, always feasible
explain: Loading a 5GB model from S3 to instance memory at cold start can take 30-120 seconds depending on instance storage speed, far exceeding a 30-second SLA. Solutions include quantization to reduce model size or configuring minimum instance counts to pre-warm endpoints.

q: A model deployed for customer recommendation shows bias: recommendations favor products from majority-owned companies. How should this be diagnosed and fixed?
options: Remove all recommendations
Bias is unavoidable in ML
Use SageMaker Clarify to analyze bias in recommendations; retrain with fairness constraints or adjust business rules
Ignore bias if accuracy is high
explain: SageMaker Clarify's bias analysis identifies disparate impact in recommendation outputs across product ownership groups. Retraining with fairness constraints or adjusting business rules addresses systemic bias without sacrificing recommendation quality.

q: An ML engineer prepares data for a time-series model by creating windows of 30 consecutive days as features predicting the next day's target. A sliding window approach is used. What is a potential issue?
options: No issues; windows are independent
Sliding windows improve accuracy
Windows don't affect data independence
Overlapping windows in train/validation/test cause data leakage; validation/test windows may contain training data
explain: Sliding windows with step=1 create highly overlapping windows; validation windows containing days also in training windows cause data leakage. Strict temporal splits with non-overlapping boundaries (train before split date, validation/test after) prevent this.

q: An ML engineer fine-tunes a BERT model on a domain-specific dataset (medical texts). The task is medical entity recognition. Which loss function should be used?
options: Binary cross-entropy (BCL)
Categorical cross-entropy for multi-class token classification (disease, drug, symptom, etc.)
No loss function needed
Mean squared error (MSE)
explain: Named entity recognition (NER) is multi-class token classification (each token classified as disease, drug, symptom, or O); categorical cross-entropy computes loss across all entity classes per token. Binary cross-entropy is for binary tasks; MSE is for regression.

q: An organization uses multi-model SageMaker endpoints where multiple models share the same instance for cost savings. When should multi-model endpoints be preferred over single-model?
options: Always use multi-model
Never use multi-model
Multi-model when models have similar response characteristics and traffic patterns; shared instance utilization is efficient
Model consolidation is not possible
explain: SageMaker Multi-Model Endpoints load and unload models dynamically from S3 into shared memory; they are most efficient when models serve requests at different times (complementary traffic patterns), enabling high utilization. Models with identical peak traffic contend for shared resources.

q: A model's prediction latency has gradually increased from 50ms to 200ms over 6 months. The team never updated the model. What could cause this?
options: This is normal
The model complexity increased
The instance type changed
Data drift may require model retraining; stale models sometimes accumulate more compute as input distributions change
explain: Gradual latency increase over 6 months with unchanged model code typically indicates resource contention — increasing request volume causing instance CPU/memory saturation. Endpoint scaling review or instance type upgrade resolves queue-induced latency growth.

q: An ML engineer prepares data where timestamps are in milliseconds since epoch. Should these raw values be used as model features?
options: No; derive meaningful features like hour, day, month, or encode cyclicity; raw timestamps don't capture temporal patterns
Timestamps should never be used
Yes, raw timestamps are numerical and should work
Timestamps are always interpretable
explain: Raw epoch timestamps (e.g., 1704067200000ms) are arbitrary large numbers that models cannot interpret meaningfully; derived features like hour-of-day, day-of-week, and is_weekend capture the periodic patterns that are actually predictive.

q: An ML engineer evaluates two models using cross-validation. Model A: mean CV accuracy=85%, std=15%. Model B: mean CV accuracy=83%, std=2%. Which model is more reliable?
options: Model A due to higher mean
Variance doesn't matter
Model B due to lower variance across folds; consistent performance is more reliable than high variance
Both are equally reliable
explain: Model B's lower cross-validation variance (std=2%) indicates consistent performance across all data subsets, making it more reliable in production. Model A's high variance (std=15%) suggests performance is highly sensitive to the specific training samples seen.

q: An ML engineer needs to update a SageMaker endpoint's model without causing downtime. The new model is ready in Model Registry. How can this be done safely?
options: Deploy to separate endpoint and switch DNS
Update endpoint immediately, causing brief downtime
Manual transition of requests
Update endpoint with shadow variant, route subset of traffic, verify, then swap traffic weights
explain: Shadow variants route a copy of live traffic to the new model without affecting user experience; the new model's predictions are logged but not served to users. This enables A/B comparison on real traffic before any production cutover.

q: An organization trains on data with strong seasonality (e.g., retail sales spike during holidays). A simple train/test split by random sampling may result in test set with different seasonal composition. What split strategy is better?
options: Combine all seasons in training
Ignore seasonality in splits
Random split is fine
Chronological split ensures test set has representative seasonal periods
explain: Chronological splits ensure the test set contains data from all seasonal periods in production, providing realistic evaluation across seasonal variations. Random splits mix seasons between train/test, creating overly optimistic test metrics.

q: An ML engineer works with an organizational data catalog (AWS Glue Catalog). A dataset lacks documentation. How should this be addressed?
options: Create separate documentation in Word
Document schema, data quality, update frequency, and business context in Glue Catalog; add tags for discoverability
Documentation is unnecessary
Use undocumented data anyway
explain: AWS Glue Data Catalog supports rich metadata: schema definitions, table descriptions, custom tags (team, domain, SLA), and business context documentation. This enables data discovery, governance, and appropriate reuse across ML teams.

q: An ML engineer applies label smoothing to a classification model where class labels are one-hot encoded. Instead of [0,1,0], labels become [0.05, 0.9, 0.05]. How does this help?
options: Label smoothing prevents overconfidence, improves generalization, and enables better uncertainty estimates
It makes the model less accurate
No effect on training
Smoothing is only for regression
explain: Label smoothing replaces hard one-hot targets with soft distributions (0.05/0.9/0.05), preventing the model from becoming overconfident and improving calibration. Well-calibrated models produce better uncertainty estimates critical for downstream decision-making.

q: An ML engineer wants to replicate a SageMaker endpoint across multiple AWS regions for disaster recovery and low-latency global serving. What is the best approach?
options: Manual replication in each region
Replication is not possible
Deploy to one region only
Register model in Model Registry; use CloudFormation or Terraform to deploy endpoints across regions consistently
explain: SageMaker Model Registry provides a central source of truth for model versions; CloudFormation or Terraform templates define endpoint configurations per region, enabling consistent, reproducible multi-region deployments from a single model artifact.

q: A deployed model shows good validation performance but poor production performance. The team investigates and finds that production data has different feature distributions than training data. What is this called?
options: Overfitting
Covariate shift or feature drift; model performs poorly when input distribution changes
Underfitting
Model corruption
explain: Covariate shift occurs when P(X) — the input feature distribution — changes but P(Y|X) — the conditional relationship — remains constant. Different feature distributions in production reduce model performance on the new distribution.

q: An ML engineer applies standard scaling (z-score normalization) to features before training a tree-based model (random forest). Is this necessary?
options: Yes, always normalize before any model
Scaling helps tree models slightly
Scaling has unknown effect on trees
No; tree-based models are invariant to feature scaling (they use splits, not distances)
explain: Tree-based models make decisions based on feature value thresholds; monotonic scaling transformations do not change relative orderings or split points, making scaling irrelevant. Distance-based models (SVM, KNN, neural networks) require scaling.

q: An ML engineer uses random search for hyperparameter tuning instead of grid search. With 5 hyperparameters and 3 values each, grid search tests 243 combinations while random search tests 50. What is the advantage of random search here?
options: Grid search is always better
Random search is only faster, not better
Random search with 50 samples covers parameter space more efficiently than small grid; often finds better solutions with fewer trials
Both are identical
explain: Random search with 50 trials samples from the full continuous parameter space and often finds good configurations faster than grid search systematically covering fewer points in high-dimensional spaces. Bayesian optimization is even more efficient.

q: An ML engineer receives a request to deploy a model predicting medical diagnosis. The predictions will influence treatment decisions. What governance step is critical before deployment?
options: Skip validation to save time
Only check training accuracy
Deploy immediately without checks
Validate model accuracy on external test set; conduct bias analysis; document limitations and get clinical review approval
explain: High-stakes medical models require validation on an external test set, SageMaker Clarify bias analysis across patient demographics, documentation of model limitations, and clinical expert review before deployment. Skipping validation (option A) violates FDA and medical device regulations.

q: An ML engineer extracts features from customer account lifetime (10 years of transaction data). The feature set becomes very large. What technique can reduce dimensionality while preserving information?
options: Dimensionality cannot be reduced
Use PCA, feature selection, or domain knowledge to select top predictive features; eliminate redundant/correlated features
Use all features regardless of size
Remove all features
explain: Feature selection (mutual information, chi-squared tests) identifies the most predictive features from 10 years of transactions, while PCA reduces correlated features into orthogonal components. Both reduce dimensionality while preserving predictive information.

q: An ML engineer trains a model and observes that training loss decreases smoothly but validation loss increases after epoch 10. What is the appropriate action?
options: Continue training to epoch 100
Increase regularization retroactively
Decrease learning rate
Use early stopping at epoch 10 to prevent overfitting; the increasing validation loss indicates no further improvement
explain: Early stopping monitors validation loss and halts training when it stops improving; the best model checkpoint (epoch 10) is saved and returned. Continuing training (option A) beyond this point only overfits to training data.

q: An ML team manages 30 SageMaker endpoints with different models. The business requires that any model update be reviewed and approved before deployment. How should this process be automated?
options: Manual approval for each endpoint
Use SageMaker Model Registry with approval workflows; CI/CD pipeline runs tests and gates deployment on approval
Skip approval to speed deployment
Approval is not possible
explain: SageMaker Model Registry approval workflows enforce a human approval gate before deployment; CI/CD pipelines run automated quality tests and block deployment until all gates pass. Manual approval per endpoint (option A) is the bottleneck alternative but Registry approval is the scalable automated process.

q: A credit scoring model is monitored and shows no change in accuracy metrics for 6 months. However, the approval rate for loan applications has increased 10%. What could explain this without accuracy changing?
options: Accuracy guarantees approval rate stability
Prediction distribution may have shifted (more high-score predictions); threshold adjustment or business rule changes shifted approval rate
This is impossible
The model is unchanged
explain: Approval rate changes without model retraining indicate prediction distribution shift (model scoring applicants higher overall) or business rule changes (threshold adjustment). This requires investigation into what caused the distribution shift.

q: An ML engineer prepares historical customer data for training. Some records contain PII (personally identifiable information) that should be removed before sharing with ML teams. What is the best approach?
options: Store PII separately without linking to data
Use SageMaker Data Wrangler or AWS Glue DataBrew to mask/redact PII while preserving useful information
Leave PII in data; ML teams need all information
Remove entire records with PII
explain: SageMaker Data Wrangler and AWS Glue DataBrew provide automated PII detection and masking recipes (name hashing, email anonymization, SSN redaction), enabling compliant data sharing. Keeping PII intact (option C) violates GDPR and privacy regulations.

q: An ML engineer trains a transformer model on 10 million sentences. The model has 300M parameters. Gradient accumulation is used with effective batch size of 10,000. Why use gradient accumulation?
options: Reduces model accuracy
Simulates large batch training on memory-limited hardware; improves stability and allows larger effective batch size
Accumulation has no benefit
Slower convergence
explain: Gradient accumulation sums gradients over N micro-batches before updating weights, simulating a batch size N times larger than fits in GPU memory. This enables BERT-scale training on hardware with limited memory without sacrificing the convergence benefits of large effective batch sizes.

q: An ML engineer deploys models in a production environment with strict security requirements: no internet access, encrypted data at rest/in-transit. How should SageMaker endpoints be configured?
options: Deploy in private VPC subnet; use S3 VPC endpoint for model artifact access; encrypt artifacts and network traffic
Deploy to public subnet with internet access
Security requirements are incompatible with SageMaker
Skip encryption for performance
explain: SageMaker endpoints deployed in private VPC subnets with no internet route satisfy no-internet requirements; S3 VPC gateway endpoints provide private access to model artifacts; KMS encryption handles data at rest/in-transit. Public subnets (option B) violate the security constraint.

q: A model is deployed and monitored. SageMaker Model Monitor shows that the model is predicting values outside the training data range (e.g., predicting salary >$10M when training max was $500K). What should be done?
options: Disable the model
Investigate if this indicates concept drift or if the model learned an undesirable extrapolation; consider retraining or adding constraints
This is normal
Accept the predictions
explain: Out-of-range predictions may indicate extrapolation to unseen input distributions; investigation determines if this reflects model errors, distributional shift, or legitimate new data patterns requiring retraining with extended ranges.

q: An ML engineer handles a dataset with extreme outliers: most features are in [0, 100] but occasional values reach 10,000. Should outliers be removed or transformed?
options: Outliers have no impact on models
Investigate outliers (are they errors or real?); if real, apply robust scaling (RobustScaler) that's less sensitive to extremes
Keep all outliers without addressing
Always remove outliers
explain: RobustScaler uses median and IQR instead of mean/std, making it resilient to extreme outliers. Investigating outliers first determines if they are measurement errors (remove) or valid extreme cases (retain with robust scaling).

q: An ML team builds a churn prediction model. Training data includes customer behavior from 2021. Will this model work well on 2024 data without retraining?
options: Always retrain monthly regardless
Retraining is never needed
Unlikely; customer preferences and business context evolve; model will experience concept drift and should be retrained periodically
Yes, customer behavior is stable over time
explain: Customer behavior changes significantly over 3 years (new products, lifestyle shifts, economic changes); concept drift causes model predictions to degrade. SageMaker Model Monitor can detect when retraining is needed rather than retraining blindly on a fixed monthly schedule.

q: An ML engineer trains a recommendation model that uses user embeddings and item embeddings learned jointly. After training, the engineer can use these embeddings for downstream tasks. This approach is called:
options: Feature engineering only
It's not possible to reuse embeddings
Representation learning or transfer learning; embeddings capture learned features for reuse in other tasks
Embeddings are only for the original task
explain: Jointly trained user and item embeddings capture latent preference structures that transfer to other recommendation tasks, content similarity, clustering, and search. These embeddings encode more information than manually engineered features.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model to production and discovers it requires custom inference code (pre/post-processing). Should this be packaged in a custom container or as SageMaker SDK code?
options: Custom container only
Pre/post-processing is not possible
Package custom code in Docker container and deploy via SageMaker; ensures reproducibility and handles pre/post-processing
Custom code on user's application side only
explain: Custom Docker containers encapsulate the model, dependencies, and pre/post-processing logic, ensuring reproducibility across environments. Packaging inference code separately from the model container (option D) creates deployment complexity and dependency management issues.

q: A healthcare prediction model's decisions are logged for compliance. An audit requires: 'Explain why this patient was classified as high-risk.' The team should use:
options: Patient's raw features only
Tell auditors the model is accurate
SageMaker Clarify SHAP explanations showing which features drove the high-risk prediction for that specific patient
The model cannot be explained
explain: SageMaker Clarify SHAP explanations attribute each feature's contribution to a specific prediction, enabling clinicians and auditors to understand exactly which patient attributes drove the high-risk classification.

q: An ML team works with large unstructured text documents (contracts, reports). Before ML training, what is the essential first step?
options: Text is unsuitable for ML
Preprocess: remove metadata/formatting, tokenize, clean noise; extract structured features or embeddings for training
Remove all documents >1 page
Use text directly in models
explain: Unstructured text requires preprocessing (metadata removal, noise cleaning, tokenization) before ML training to convert it to usable numerical representations. Text cannot be fed directly to most ML algorithms without feature extraction steps.

q: An ML engineer trains a neural network but notices that gradients during backpropagation become very small (vanishing gradients). This prevents early layers from learning. How can this be addressed?
options: Vanishing gradients are unavoidable
Use batch normalization or residual connections to stabilize gradient flow through deep networks
Increase learning rate to compensate
Use a shallower network only
explain: Batch normalization normalizes layer inputs to prevent internal covariate shift, maintaining gradient magnitudes through deep networks. Residual connections provide gradient highways that bypass problematic layers, both addressing vanishing gradients in deep architectures.

q: An ML engineer manages endpoints using SageMaker Pipelines for continuous deployment. The pipeline is triggered automatically when new training data arrives. What risk exists?
options: Continuous deployment is not possible
Continuous deployment ensures always-fresh models
No risk with automation
Risk of deploying broken/biased models; pipeline should include automated tests (accuracy, bias checks) before deployment
explain: Automated deployment pipelines without quality gates risk deploying models with data quality issues, bias, or bugs. Accuracy threshold checks, bias analysis, and latency tests are essential gates that prevent degraded models from reaching production.

q: An ML model's predictions are used to decide which job candidates to interview. The model is monitored and is found to be biased: 60% of majority group candidates are recommended vs 40% for minority group. What is the issue?
options: Disparate impact (different outcomes for protected groups); use SageMaker Clarify to quantify bias and retrain with fairness objectives
This is acceptable if accuracy is high
Bias is unavoidable
Only accuracy matters
explain: A 60% vs 40% recommendation rate difference for majority vs minority groups with identical qualifications is disparate impact, violating equal employment opportunity principles. SageMaker Clarify quantifies this disparity, and retraining with fairness objectives addresses the bias.

q: An ML engineer ingests time-series data with seasonal patterns (e.g., daily temperature). To train a forecasting model, what is critical?
options: Seasonality is irrelevant for time-series
Remove seasonal patterns as noise
Preserve seasonality by not removing trends; explicitly model seasonal components or use seasonal decomposition
Combine all seasons into single trend
explain: Seasonal decomposition (STL, classical decomposition) separates trend, seasonal, and residual components, enabling explicit modeling of seasonal patterns. Removing seasonal patterns (option B) destroys predictive information critical for accurate forecasting.

q: An ML engineer trains a model on heavily imbalanced data (1% positive class). The model achieves 99% accuracy. What metric should the engineer focus on instead?
options: Inference speed
Accuracy is the right metric
Training loss
F1-score, precision-recall curve, or ROC-AUC focus on minority class performance and avoid misleading high accuracy
explain: On 1% positive class data, a classifier predicting all negatives achieves 99% accuracy; F1-score averages precision and recall for the minority class, and ROC-AUC measures discriminability independent of threshold, both being meaningful metrics for imbalanced classification.

q: An ML team uses GitOps for model deployment. A developer accidentally commits a model with poor performance. How should this be prevented?
options: Skip validation to speed deployment
No checks; deploy immediately
Implement pre-commit hooks and CI/CD validation to test model performance before merging
Manual review only
explain: CI/CD pre-commit hooks and pipeline validation enforce performance thresholds before merging — models below accuracy baseline or exceeding latency limits are rejected. PR code reviews catch architectural issues; automated tests verify correctness.

q: A deployed model is monitored using SageMaker Model Monitor. The baseline report shows: model accuracy 85%. New evaluation shows: accuracy 83%. Should the model be retrained?
options: Yes, immediately retrain
Accuracy changes are not monitored
No, investigate: 2% drop may be normal variance; check if statistical significance, drift type, or sample size explains it
Ignore the drop
explain: A 2% accuracy drop (85% to 83%) is within normal measurement variance; statistical significance testing with confidence intervals determines whether it represents real degradation or sampling noise. Immediate retraining (option A) based on noise wastes resources.

q: An ML team discovers that the same customer appears multiple times in training data with different IDs (data duplication). How should this be handled?
options: Deduplicate based on identifying information; ensure train/test don't share customers (prevent leakage)
Duplicates improve model generalization
Ignore duplication
Keep duplicates to increase dataset size
explain: Duplicate records inflate dataset size without adding information and create train-test leakage if the same customer appears in both sets. Entity resolution and deduplication should precede any train/test split.

q: An ML engineer uses a pre-trained language model for a new task. The model is fine-tuned on small task-specific data (100 examples). What regularization is important?
options: Regularization harms transfer learning
No regularization needed for fine-tuning
Remove all constraints to maximize accuracy
Strong regularization (dropout, L2, early stopping) to prevent overfitting on small fine-tuning dataset
explain: Fine-tuning pre-trained models on 100 examples is highly prone to overfitting; strong dropout, L2 weight decay, and early stopping preserve pre-trained representations while adapting to the new task without memorizing the small training set.

q: An ML engineer wants to serve a model on edge devices with limited compute (ARM CPU, 256MB RAM). The original model is 500MB. What approach enables this?
options: Edge deployment is not possible
Deploy full model anyway
Use model compression: quantization (int8), pruning, distillation into smaller student models
Larger models always perform better
explain: Model compression via INT8 quantization (4x size reduction), magnitude pruning, and knowledge distillation can reduce a 500MB model to fit in 256MB RAM while maintaining acceptable accuracy for edge deployment.

q: A fraud detection model is deployed. The business team reports: 'False positive rate increased from 1% to 5%.' This causes too many legitimate transactions to be flagged. What should be done?
options: Investigate: lowering decision threshold increased false positives; retrain with cost-sensitive learning or adjust threshold
Ignore the increase
Deploy a new random model
Increase threshold arbitrarily
explain: False positive rate increase (1% to 5%) suggests the decision threshold is too low or the model's positive class scores have inflated. Threshold calibration or cost-sensitive retraining that penalizes false positives more heavily addresses this.

q: An ML engineer applies SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) to generate synthetic samples for the minority class. After applying SMOTE to training data, should it be applied to validation/test data?
options: No; SMOTE only on training data to create realistic synthetic examples; validation/test remain unchanged and representative
SMOTE should be skipped entirely
Yes, apply SMOTE to all data
Test data should be oversampled more than training
explain: SMOTE generates synthetic minority examples by interpolating between real minority samples; applying it to test data would contaminate evaluation with synthetic patterns not present in real production data, giving artificially optimistic performance estimates.

q: An ML engineer trains a model with batch size 128. On the same hardware with batch size 256, training converges faster but final accuracy is lower. What is happening?
options: Larger batch sizes reduce variance in gradient estimates (faster convergence) but may get stuck in sharper minima (lower accuracy)
Lower accuracy is impossible with larger batch
Larger batch size always improves both speed and accuracy
Batch size doesn't affect convergence
explain: Larger batches (256 vs 128) average gradients over more samples per update, reducing gradient noise and enabling larger steps — converging faster. However, they can converge to sharper minima with lower generalization, causing slightly lower final accuracy.

q: An ML team manages models in SageMaker Model Registry. A model should not be deployed until it passes:  accuracy >90%, no bias (fairness metric >0.8), latency <100ms. How should this be enforced?
options: Manual checks by ML team
Skip checks to speed deployment
Checks are not enforceable
Automation: define SageMaker Pipelines conditional steps that test these criteria before deploying; only deploy if all pass
explain: SageMaker Pipelines ConditionStep evaluates model accuracy, bias metrics, and latency checks; only if all three thresholds pass does the pipeline proceed to deployment. This prevents any single failing check from allowing a substandard model to reach production.

q: An ML engineer works with customer behavioral data across multiple time zones. Timestamps are in UTC. What is important for feature extraction?
options: Time zones have no effect on features
Consider customer's local timezone when extracting time-based features (hour of day, day of week) to match local context
Ignore time zones; use UTC directly
Convert all to single timezone arbitrarily
explain: Temporal features should reflect the customer's local timezone to capture local behavioral patterns (peak shopping hours, weekend behavior). UTC hour features are meaningless for a customer in Tokyo where 9am UTC is 6pm local time.

q: An ML engineer trains a model on data from 2020-2022. In 2023, the model's performance drops significantly. The team checks: are features different? Yes. Are targets different? No. What is this?
options: Concept drift
Model bug
Overfitting
Covariate shift (data drift): input distribution changed but input-output relationship remains the same
explain: Covariate shift is defined as P(X) changing while P(Y|X) remains constant — the input distribution changed (different features in 2023) but the relationship between features and target is the same. This differs from concept drift where P(Y|X) changes.

q: An ML engineer deploys models to SageMaker endpoints for 50 different customer segments, each with its own model. Managing 50 endpoints individually is tedious. How can this scale?
options: Eliminate models
Register all models in Model Registry with segment tags; use SageMaker Pipelines to deploy/update all segment models in parallel
Consolidate to single model
Manually manage each endpoint
explain: Model Registry with segment tags enables querying all segment models at once; SageMaker Pipelines parallel steps can deploy/update multiple models simultaneously, reducing management overhead from O(n) manual steps to a single pipeline execution.

q: A model predicts which students will drop out of college. The model is audited and found to systematically underpredict dropout for low-income students. What is the issue?
options: This is a systematic bias (disparate impact for low-income group); the model discriminates based on a proxy for protected status
Bias doesn't exist if accuracy is high
Fairness checks are unnecessary
Model accuracy is still high, so it's acceptable
explain: Systematic underprediction of dropout for low-income students is disparate impact — the model disadvantages a group correlated with protected attributes. SageMaker Clarify quantifies this disparity; fairness-aware retraining or income-debiased features addresses it.

q: An ML engineer works with tabular data containing both numeric and categorical features. The categorical feature 'country' has 195 unique values. Should one-hot encoding be used?
options: Yes, one-hot encode all categories
Remove categorical features entirely
No; one-hot encoding 195 categories creates 195 sparse columns; use target encoding, label encoding, or embedding approaches
Combine countries into regions
explain: One-hot encoding 195 countries creates 195 sparse binary columns, causing curse of dimensionality. Target encoding replaces each country with its mean target value (single compact feature). Embedding layers learn dense country representations in neural networks.

q: An ML engineer evaluates a model using 5-fold cross-validation. Fold 1 accuracy: 90%, Fold 2: 88%, Fold 3: 92%, Fold 4: 85%, Fold 5: 89%. What is the CV accuracy and what does the variance tell us?
options: All folds have identical accuracy
CV accuracy = 88%, high variance indicates unstable model performance across data subsets
CV accuracy = 88.8%, variance of 2.6 indicates stable performance across folds
Variance is irrelevant
explain: Mean CV accuracy = (90+88+92+85+89)/5 = 88.8%; standard deviation of approximately 2.6% indicates stable performance across all five folds, suggesting the model generalizes consistently regardless of which data subset is used for training.

q: An ML team uses SageMaker Pipelines for automated model deployment. The pipeline runs daily, retrains, evaluates, and conditionally deploys. What should be monitored to ensure reliability?
options: Nothing; pipelines are self-sufficient
Monitor pipeline execution logs, failure rates, model quality metrics (accuracy drift), and deployment success; alert on failures
Skip monitoring to reduce overhead
Pipelines cannot fail
explain: Automated pipelines require monitoring pipeline execution health (failure rate, duration), model quality metrics, and deployment outcomes to detect silent failures. Treating pipelines as self-sufficient (option A) leads to undetected degradation in production.

q: A model is deployed and serves 1M requests/day. SageMaker Model Monitor detects data drift. The team wants to understand: 'Which features are drifting?' What should be checked?
options: Drift cannot be attributed to specific features
Overall accuracy only
Model Monitor reports feature-level statistics (mean, distribution) comparing baseline to recent data; identify which features drifted
All features drift equally
explain: SageMaker Model Monitor provides per-feature statistics (mean, standard deviation, distribution histograms) comparing production data vs. baseline, identifying which specific features have drifted. Overall accuracy alone (option B) doesn't reveal which features are causing the performance change.

q: An ML engineer builds a dataset for sentiment analysis containing product reviews of varying lengths (10-5000 words). How should text length variation be handled during training?
options: Use only the first 10 words of each review
Remove all reviews longer than 512 tokens
Keep variable lengths; use padding/truncation to uniform length for batch processing
Text length is irrelevant
explain: Padding adds zeros to shorter sequences and truncation cuts longer sequences to create uniform-length batches for efficient GPU processing. Padding masks indicate real vs. padded tokens, preventing the model from learning patterns in padding tokens.

q: An organization's customer database contains millions of records with occasional missing values in key features. An ML engineer must decide: drop rows with missing values or impute them?
options: Always drop rows with any missing values
Replace all missing values with 0
Impute using mean/median/mode for numeric features; use categorical mode or create 'missing' category for categorical features
Missing values should remain
explain: Imputation (mean/median for numeric, mode or 'missing' indicator for categorical) preserves sample count and avoids throwing away records that are otherwise complete. Dropping all rows with missing values (option A) is only appropriate when missingness rate is very high and imputation would introduce significant bias.

q: An ML engineer trains a model with 1000 epochs but uses early stopping with patience=10. What does patience=10 mean?
options: Train exactly 1000 epochs
Stop training if validation loss doesn't improve for 10 consecutive epochs; prevents overfitting without arbitrary epoch limits
Patience has no effect
Stop after 10 epochs
explain: Patience=10 means training stops if the validation metric doesn't improve for 10 consecutive epochs, then restores weights from the best epoch. Stopping after exactly 10 epochs (option D) regardless of improvement is just a fixed epoch limit, not early stopping.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model for real-time fraud detection. The inference latency is 80ms, but SLA requires <50ms. What options exist?
options: Accept the latency violation
Optimize model (quantization, pruning), upgrade instance type, or cache predictions to meet latency SLA
Disable monitoring
Latency cannot be improved
explain: Model optimization (INT8 quantization, operator fusion, TensorRT compilation) reduces inference compute time; faster instance types reduce per-inference latency; prediction caching serves repeated requests instantly. Accepting the latency violation (option A) breaches the SLA.

q: A deployed model's decision thresholds are: predict positive if P(positive) > 0.5. An audit reveals that this threshold results in unequal false positive rates across demographic groups. What is the solution?
options: Keep threshold at 0.5
Demographic groups should not be considered
Use fairness-aware threshold selection: choose threshold that equalized false positive rate or false negative rate across groups
Thresholds cannot be adjusted
explain: Fairness-aware threshold selection applies different decision thresholds per demographic group to equalize false positive (or false negative) rates, satisfying equalized odds fairness constraints. A single global threshold at 0.5 (option A) creates unequal error rates across groups.

q: An ML engineer works with categorical features that have hundreds of unique values. One-hot encoding creates a sparse matrix. What is a more efficient approach?
options: Sparse matrices are always best
Remove all categorical features
Use embedding layers (for neural networks) or label encoding with tree models; target encoding encodes by target mean
Use all one-hot encoded features
explain: Embedding layers learn dense, compact representations (e.g., 32-dimensional) for hundreds of categorical values; target encoding computes the mean target value per category. Both are far more efficient than one-hot encoding, which creates hundreds of sparse binary columns.

q: An ML engineer trains a model using stochastic gradient descent (SGD) with a fixed learning rate of 0.01. Training plateaus at 80% accuracy after 100 epochs and doesn't improve. What should be tried?
options: Use learning rate decay or reduce learning rate; try different optimizers (Adam) that adapt learning rate
Continue training for 1000 epochs
Stop immediately
Increase learning rate
explain: When training plateaus at 80% with a fixed learning rate, applying learning rate decay or switching to Adam (which adapts per-parameter learning rates) can help escape the local minimum. Continuing for 1000 more epochs (option B) with the same LR is unlikely to improve if the loss surface has been fully explored.

q: An ML team deploys models to multiple regions for global availability. Models must be kept in sync across regions. How should this be managed?
options: Manually deploy to each region
Regional sync is not possible
Deploy to only one region
Register models in central Model Registry; replicate across regions using CloudFormation or Terraform
explain: Registering the authoritative model version in a central Model Registry and using CloudFormation/Terraform to deploy consistent endpoints across regions ensures version synchronization and prevents drift between regional deployments.

q: A lending model is monitored and shows: new loan approval rate 45%, historical approval rate 30%. No retraining occurred. What could explain this approval rate change?
options: Model parameters changed automatically
Model accuracy improved
This is impossible without retraining
Prediction distribution shifted (higher predicted scores); decision threshold remained fixed; or business rule changed approval criteria
explain: Without model retraining, loan approval rate can change if the input distribution shifted (applicants now have higher credit scores), causing more high-score predictions at a fixed threshold. A business rule threshold change would also shift approval rates without model changes.

q: An ML engineer extracts time-based features from transaction timestamps: hour, day of week, is_weekend. Some customers transact 24/7, others only business hours. What feature engineering step is important?
options: Extract customer-specific patterns: customer's peak transaction hour, typical day of week; personalize features by customer
Ignore customer behavior
All customers have same patterns
Use raw timestamps
explain: Customer-specific temporal patterns (individual's typical transaction hour, weekday preference) are more predictive than global time features, as different customers have different behavioral rhythms. Raw timestamps are not interpretable as features without aggregation.

q: An ML engineer trains a classification model and observes: training loss = 0.1, validation loss = 0.3. The model shows signs of overfitting. What regularization technique is most likely to help?
options: Add L2 regularization, dropout, or data augmentation to reduce model complexity and improve generalization
Increase model size
Remove all regularization
Regularization harms accuracy
explain: Training loss 0.1 vs. validation loss 0.3 indicates overfitting; L2 regularization penalizes large weights, dropout prevents co-adaptation, and data augmentation expands effective training set size. Removing regularization (option C) would worsen the training-validation gap.

q: An ML engineer manages SageMaker endpoints across different environments (dev, staging, prod). What strategy ensures consistency?
options: Manual management per environment
Different models per environment
Register models in Model Registry; use environment-specific deployment configs (instance types, scaling) from single model version
Consistency is not possible
explain: Model Registry stores a single validated model version deployed consistently across dev, staging, and production; environment-specific endpoint configurations (instance types, auto-scaling, VPC settings) separate model quality concerns from deployment configuration.

q: A deployed model for recommendation is monitored. Metrics show high precision but low recall. Many good recommendations are missed. What is the issue?
options: Recall cannot be improved without retraining
Precision and recall are independent
Low recall means the model is conservative; consider lowering decision threshold to recommend more items and improve recall
Increase precision further
explain: High precision with low recall means the model only recommends items it is very confident about, missing many good recommendations. Lowering the recommendation score threshold increases recall at the cost of slightly lower precision (some less-relevant items included).

q: An ML engineer works with sequential event data (user actions: view, click, purchase, return). To train a model predicting the next action, what sequence length should be used?
options: Use fixed window (e.g., last 10 events) as features; balance recency with sufficient context for predictions
Use only the most recent event
Sequence length doesn't matter
Use all historical events (years of data) per user
explain: Fixed-length sequence windows (e.g., last 10-50 events) balance computational efficiency with sufficient behavioral context. Using all historical events (option D) creates variable-length sequences requiring heavy padding for very old interactions that may not be predictive.

q: An ML engineer trains a model on balanced data (50% positive, 50% negative). The model achieves 90% accuracy. The engineer deploys it to production where true data is 90% negative, 10% positive. Will performance generalize?
options: No; the model was trained on different class distribution; accuracy will degrade; use class weights or rebalance training data
Deploy without concern
Accuracy is independent of class distribution
Yes, accuracy will remain 90%
explain: A model trained on 50/50 balanced data learns calibrated probabilities for balanced classes; deployed on 90/10 imbalanced production data, its decision threshold is miscalibrated. Recalibrating probabilities or retraining with class weights matching production distribution is required.

q: An ML engineer exports a PyTorch model to ONNX for inference on CPU. The ONNX model runs 50% slower than expected. What could be the issue?
options: CPU inference lacks GPU optimizations; check ONNX operator support; consider using optimized runtime (ORT)
Export failed silently
Slowness is unavoidable
ONNX is always faster
explain: ONNX Runtime (ORT) provides CPU-optimized graph execution plans and can apply operator fusion and INT8 quantization; some ONNX operators may have suboptimal CPU implementations that profiling reveals. ONNX is not always faster (option D) — it depends on runtime and optimization level.

q: A model is monitored and shows 99% accuracy on test data but 60% accuracy on production data from a different source. What is the most likely issue?
options: Significant distribution shift between test and production data; model learned patterns specific to test distribution
This is normal
Production data is corrupted
Test data is unrealistic
explain: A 99%-to-60% accuracy drop between test and production data is severe distribution shift — the model learned patterns specific to the test distribution that do not generalize. This indicates the test set was not a representative sample of real-world production data.

q: An ML engineer prepares data for a time-series model. The raw data has hourly frequency, but business questions are at daily granularity. Should the engineer resample?
options: Resample to daily frequency (aggregate hourly data); match granularity to business questions and reduce noise
Keep hourly frequency
Upsample to minute frequency
Frequency doesn't matter
explain: Resampling hourly data to daily frequency by aggregation (sum, mean, max) reduces noise and aligns feature granularity with business questions. Keeping hourly frequency (option B) adds unnecessary dimensionality and noise when daily predictions are the target.

q: An ML engineer uses gradient boosting and observes that adding more trees beyond 100 stops improving validation accuracy. What does this suggest?
options: Model has converged; adding trees causes overfitting; use early stopping to prevent overfitting
Tree count doesn't affect accuracy
Delete earlier trees
Always use many more trees
explain: When validation accuracy plateaus after adding more gradient boosting trees, the model has converged; continuing to add trees overfits training data without validation improvement. Early stopping in XGBoost/LightGBM monitors validation metric and halts at the optimal number of trees.

q: An ML engineer deploys an endpoint with auto-scaling enabled. During low traffic periods, instances scale down. A request arrives and the endpoint has zero instances. What happens?
options: Request is immediately served
Auto-scaling prevents this
Request waits for instance launch (cold start); adds latency; configure minimum capacity if cold starts are unacceptable
Request fails instantly
explain: When auto-scaling scales to zero instances and a new request arrives, SageMaker must launch a new instance and load the model before serving — this cold start can take 1-5 minutes. Configuring a minimum instance count of 1 eliminates cold starts at the cost of idle instance charges.

q: A model is monitored for fairness using SageMaker Clarify. The bias analysis shows: disparate impact ratio 0.5 (0.5x approval rate for Group B vs Group A). Is this acceptable?
options: Disparate impact is always acceptable
No; standard fairness threshold is 0.8 (80% rule); 0.5 indicates discrimination; retrain with fairness constraints
Bias cannot be measured
Yes, 0.5 ratio is acceptable
explain: The 80% rule (four-fifths rule) requires protected groups to receive outcomes at no less than 80% the rate of the advantaged group; a disparate impact ratio of 0.5 (50%) is well below this legal threshold and indicates statistically significant discrimination requiring remediation.

q: An ML engineer builds a training set from web scraping. Some data is sensitive (PII, copyrighted content). Before training, what must be done?
options: Use data as-is
Only use public data
Review data for PII and copyrighted content; redact PII and respect copyright; ensure ethical data practices
Ignore ethical concerns
explain: Web-scraped training data requires PII redaction (GDPR compliance), copyright assessment (fair use determination), and ethical review before use. Training on raw web data (option A) exposes the organization to regulatory fines and intellectual property liability.

q: An ML engineer trains a model with batch normalization and sees training loss = 0.05, validation loss = 0.5. What is the issue?
options: Severe overfitting; validation loss 10x training loss indicates model memorizes training data
This is normal
Batch norm is working correctly
Batch norm caused overfitting
explain: Training loss 0.05 vs. validation loss 0.5 is a 10x difference indicating severe overfitting — the model has completely memorized training examples. Stronger regularization (dropout, L2, early stopping) and data augmentation are needed to address this.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model using multi-model endpoints where multiple models share instance capacity. When should this be preferred over dedicated endpoints?
options: Multi-model is not supported
When model traffic is low and intermittent; shared instances increase utilization and reduce costs
Never use multi-model
Always use multi-model
explain: SageMaker Multi-Model Endpoints efficiently serve models with low, intermittent traffic by dynamically loading and unloading models from a shared container — cost is proportional to instance hours rather than one instance per model. High-traffic or latency-critical models should use dedicated endpoints.

q: A fraud model's recall decreases (fewer frauds caught) without any model changes or retraining. What could explain this?
options: Recall changes randomly
Model degraded automatically
Fraud patterns evolved (concept drift); previous features no longer detect fraud; retraining on recent data needed
Recall is constant without retraining
explain: Fraud patterns evolve as fraudsters adapt tactics; concept drift (changing P(Y|X)) causes previously effective detection features to lose discriminative power. Retraining on recent labeled fraud data restores recall by incorporating new fraud signatures.

q: An organization ingests customer support transcripts (unstructured text) for a classifier. The texts are very long (5000+ words). What is a practical approach?
options: Text classification needs short inputs
Use entire transcript as-is
Truncate to first 512 tokens, or summarize; extract key sections (problem, resolution) as features
Use only the first sentence
explain: Transformer models (BERT, RoBERTa) have maximum sequence lengths (512-2048 tokens); long support transcripts require truncation, summarization, or extracting key sections (issue description, resolution). Using entire long transcripts (option B) exceeds model capacity and dilutes relevant signal.

q: An ML engineer trains a neural network with 5 hidden layers. Training is very slow. What could speed up training?
options: Remove all hidden layers
Use batch normalization in hidden layers to stabilize training and enable higher learning rates
Train on CPU
Depth doesn't affect training speed
explain: Batch normalization normalizes layer activations, preventing internal covariate shift and enabling higher learning rates for faster convergence. It is particularly effective in deep (5+ layer) networks where gradient flow issues slow training.

q: An ML engineer manages SageMaker endpoints that serve different models for different business lines (finance, retail, healthcare). What governance structure ensures quality?
options: No governance
Governance is unnecessary
Each line manages independently
Centralized Model Registry with approval workflows; each business line has deployment checklists and SLAs
explain: A centralized SageMaker Model Registry with approval workflows, business-line-specific deployment checklists, and SLA definitions provides governance accountability across diverse model portfolios. No governance (option A) leads to untested models reaching production.

q: A deployed model's explanation (SHAP values) shows that feature X is the top driver for some predictions. However, using feature X directly is illegal. What should be done?
options: Ignore the explanation
Explanations don't inform feature selection
Continue using feature X
Remove feature X or add fairness constraints; consider if X is a proxy for protected attributes
explain: When SHAP analysis reveals a legally prohibited feature as the top driver, the feature must be removed or constrained — its continued use creates legal liability. If the feature is a proxy for protected attributes, indirect use requires fairness analysis and remediation.

q: An ML engineer creates a feature for time-series forecasting: 'day of year' (1-365). This feature is numeric but cyclical (day 1 is close to day 365). Should encoding change?
options: Remove day-of-year feature
Use sin/cos encoding to preserve cyclicity: sin(2π*day/365), cos(2π*day/365)
Cyclicity doesn't matter
Use day of year directly
explain: Sin/cos encoding maps the cyclical day-of-year to a 2D circle where day 1 and day 365 are adjacent, preserving calendar proximity. Linear day values (option D) incorrectly represent December 31 and January 1 as maximally far apart — a fundamental misrepresentation.

q: An ML engineer trains a recommendation model that predicts items a user will like. The model is evaluated on a test set and achieves high precision (90%) but low recall (10%). What is the issue?
options: Precision-recall tradeoff doesn't apply
Precision is the only metric that matters
Recall cannot be improved
Low recall means the model recommends very few items; users miss many good recommendations; increase recommendation count to improve recall
explain: 10% recall means the model correctly recommends only 10% of items users would actually like — severe under-recommendation. Increasing the recommendation count (larger K) or lowering the score threshold improves recall at modest precision cost.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model in a regulated industry (healthcare, finance) where audit trails are required. What should be logged?
options: Only model name
Input features, model version, prediction, prediction confidence, timestamp, user ID for auditing decisions
No logging needed
Logging is optional
explain: Regulated industries require full audit trails: input features, model version, prediction output, confidence score, timestamp, and user/case ID. This enables compliance auditors to trace any individual decision and verify regulatory requirements were met.

q: A model monitors its own predictions and notices the prediction distribution has shifted significantly. However, true labels haven't arrived yet (prediction lag). Should the team retrain?
options: Wait for true labels to confirm drift through performance metrics; use prediction drift as early signal, but validate before retraining
Never use prediction distribution
Drift cannot be detected without labels
Retrain immediately
explain: Prediction distribution shift is an early warning signal but retraining requires confirmation through ground truth labels when available. Without label confirmation, aggressive retraining may introduce unnecessary instability; use prediction drift to trigger closer monitoring.

q: An ML engineer processes geographic data (latitude, longitude) for a location-based recommendation model. Should raw coordinates be used as features?
options: Coordinates are always best
No; encode as distances to landmarks, grid cells, or embeddings; raw coordinates are high-dimensional and non-informative
Geographic data is unsuitable for ML
Yes, use coordinates directly
explain: Geographic encoding approaches (H3 grid cells, distance to known locations, geohash embeddings) capture spatial structure meaningfully. Raw latitude/longitude coordinates (option D) provide no context about surrounding amenities or urban density.

q: An ML engineer trains a model using k-fold cross-validation with k=10. One fold shows accuracy 95%, but other folds show 70%. What does this suggest?
options: Average accuracy is the truth
High variance across folds indicates data heterogeneity or overfitting to specific fold; investigate data issues
Model is robust
Fold variance doesn't matter
explain: High cross-validation variance (one fold at 95%, others at 70%) indicates the model's performance is highly sensitive to which data subset it was trained on — typically due to data heterogeneity, class imbalance within folds, or overfitting on the outlier fold.

q: An ML team uses Kubernetes to manage containerized model services. How should SageMaker models be integrated with Kubernetes?
options: Kubernetes cannot run models
SageMaker is incompatible with Kubernetes
Use SageMaker endpoints only
Export model to Docker container; deploy to Kubernetes; use SageMaker for training and export workflow
explain: SageMaker-trained models can be exported as Docker containers (or ONNX/TorchScript format) and deployed to Kubernetes using standard k8s Deployments and Services. SageMaker is fully compatible with Kubernetes-based inference infrastructure.

q: A model's performance degrades but the business team says: 'We intentionally changed the approval threshold to approve more cases.' The team didn't retrain. Is this acceptable?
options: No monitoring is needed
No; threshold changes alter the precision-recall tradeoff; monitor false positive/negative rates and evaluate downstream impact
Threshold changes are invisible to users
Yes, threshold changes don't affect predictions
explain: Threshold changes shift the operating point on the precision-recall curve, directly affecting false positive and false negative rates. Monitoring these downstream metrics after threshold changes ensures the business impact (customer experience, regulatory compliance) remains acceptable.

q: An ML engineer works with image data where different sources have different resolutions and color spaces (RGB, grayscale). Preprocessing is critical before training. What is required?
options: Preprocessing is optional
Convert all images to same color space and resolution; apply normalization (e.g., divide by 255 for 8-bit images)
Use images as-is
Remove images with different specs
explain: Standardizing color space (all RGB or all grayscale), resolution, and normalizing pixel values to [0,1] enables consistent batch processing in convolutional networks. Mixed formats and resolutions cause tensor shape errors or require variable-length processing.

q: An ML engineer implements a custom training loop in PyTorch instead of using Keras/TensorFlow high-level API. What is a key consideration?
options: Ensure proper gradient computation, backward pass, optimization step, and learning rate scheduling; test on small dataset first
Custom loops are always unnecessary
Custom loops have no impact on results
Debugging is impossible with custom loops
